text: Some time later Absalom got himself a chariot, horses, and fifty men to run ahead of him.

He would get up early and stand beside the road leading to the city gate, and whenever someone came with a case to bring before the king for judgment, Absalom would call out and ask what town he was from.
When the man explained his complaint, Absalom would say his claim sounded good and fair, but the king had no one appointed to hear it.
He would add that if only he were made judge in the land, anyone with a dispute could come to him and he would give them justice.
And whenever someone approached to bow down to him, Absalom would reach out, take hold of him, and kiss him instead.
He treated every Israelite who came to the king for judgment this way, and in doing so won over the loyalty of the people of Israel.
After four years had passed, Absalom said to the king, please let me go to Hebron and fulfill a vow I made to the LORD.
He explained that while he was living in Geshur he had vowed that if the LORD brought him back to Jerusalem, he would worship him there.
The king told him to go in peace, and Absalom set out for Hebron.
But Absalom sent secret messengers through all the tribes of Israel, telling them that as soon as they heard the sound of the trumpet, they should announce that Absalom had become king in Hebron.
Two hundred men from Jerusalem went along with Absalom as invited guests, having no idea what was really happening.
While he was offering sacrifices, Absalom sent for Ahithophel, David's advisor, from his hometown of Giloh, and the plot kept gaining strength as more people joined Absalom's side.
A messenger came to David and reported that the people of Israel had shifted their loyalty to Absalom.

David told all his officials in Jerusalem that they needed to flee immediately, or none of them would escape Absalom — he warned they had to move fast before Absalom could catch them and destroy the city.
His officials answered that they were ready to do whatever the king decided.
So the king left the city with his whole household following him, except for ten concubines he left behind to look after the palace.
The king and all his people went out and stopped at the last house on the way out of the city.
All his officials passed by him, along with the Cherethites, the Pelethites, and six hundred men from Gath who had followed him there, all marching past the king.
The king said to Ittai the Gittite, why are you coming with us? Go back and stay with the new king, since you are a foreigner who has already left your own homeland once.

You only arrived yesterday — why should I make you wander with me today when I don't even know where I'm going? Go back, take your people with you, and may the LORD deal kindly and faithfully with you.
But Ittai answered the king, as surely as the LORD lives and as you live, wherever you go, my lord the king, whether to live or to die, that is where your servant will be.
David told him, then come along. So Ittai the Gittite went on with all his men and all the families with him.
Everyone in the countryside wept loudly as the people passed by; the king crossed over the Kidron valley, and all the people continued on toward the wilderness.
Zadok was there too, along with all the Levites carrying the ark of God's covenant; they set the ark down, and Abiathar offered sacrifices, until everyone had finished leaving the city.
The king told Zadok to take the ark back into the city — if the LORD was pleased with him, he would bring David back to see it and its resting place again.
But if the LORD said he took no delight in him, then David was ready for God to do with him whatever seemed right.
The king also said to Zadok, aren't you a seer? Go back to the city in peace, and take your son Ahimaaz and Abiathar's son Jonathan with you.
David said he would wait at the crossing points of the wilderness until word reached him from them.
So Zadok and Abiathar carried the ark of God back to Jerusalem and stayed there.
David continued up the slope of the Mount of Olives, weeping the whole way, his head covered and his feet bare; everyone with him covered their heads too and wept as they climbed.
Someone told David that Ahithophel had joined the conspiracy with Absalom, and David prayed that the LORD would turn Ahithophel's advice into nonsense.
When David reached the top of the ridge, where it was customary to worship God, Hushai the Archite met him there with his robe torn and dust on his head.
David told him that if he came along, he would only be a burden.
But if he went back to the city and told Absalom he would now serve him just as he had served his father, he could work to undo Ahithophel's advice for David's sake.
David reminded him that the priests Zadok and Abiathar would be there too, so whatever he learned in the palace he should report to them.
Their two sons, Ahimaaz and Jonathan, were with them, and could carry word to David through them.
So Hushai, David's friend, went back into the city just as Absalom was arriving in Jerusalem.
